data.title: GLD: The Trump Factor In Gold's Rally
data.description:
The SPDR Gold Shares ETF (NYSEARCA:GLD), launched on 11/18/2004 and managed by World Gold Trust Services, LLC, is an ETF that tracks the performance of gold bullion. GLD manages $81.85B and charges an expense ratio of 0.40%. As the biggest ETF that offers exposure to the price of gold, it provides very high liquidity based on its trading volume.

There is a case to be made about having exposure through such an efficient way in the long term. But in this article, I want to examine the recent trend of gold's price relative to other assets, explore the potential factors that resulted in the rally, and assess the sustainability of the current trend by understanding how impactful such factors will continue to be based on what we know so far.

Long story short, I believe that the recent demand has more room to grow and a tilt towards gold or a short-term trade is warranted. You should, however, read the whole article to understand the drivers and, therefore, the risks of underperformance.

Disclaimer: In this article, I am not concerned about the long-term effects of the current administration's policies on the U.S. and global economy. This article doesn't discuss this matter as it's completely irrelevant for traders and investors looking to formulate an outlook for the next few months. The markets are neutral for the most part because profit is the end goal. In case my tone doesn't make it clear enough as you go through this article, keep in mind that I strive to have the same stance when I write such articles that involve monetary, fiscal, or trade policy.

Let's take things from the start.

What Is Happening To the Price of Gold?

Gold is back in vogue. And that may be thanks to U.S. President Donald Trump's proposed tariff policies that could support the current inflation trend and ignite a global trade war.

This also offers an explanation of why other asset classes have performed so poorly; not just relative to gold but on an absolute basis during a time when inflation hovers around 3%.

YTD, gold holds the title as one of the best-performing assets since it has increased by almost 12%. In comparison, the U.S. dollar has gone down relative to a basket of major currencies and the 10-year Treasury price has had a modest increase; despite the safe haven status of 10-year Treasuries, it can't do much when inflation and interest rate expectations shift upwards so fast. The S&P 500 and even Bitcoin have been significantly underperforming gold lately as well, indicating risk appetite is not as strong as in the recent past.

This phenomenon is a reflection of the hedge quality that gold enjoys during times of expectations for higher geopolitical tensions and market volatility.

The Israel-Hamas conflict has de-escalated but is not resolved yet. The Russia-Ukraine peace agreement is yet to come. And a potential trade war as a result of new tariffs on imported goods in the U.S. further fuels uncertainty. Such a scenario could trigger higher upward pressure on inflation and introduce a challenge to economic growth.

The recent gold shipments from the Bank of England's vaults to New York also display an interesting correlation as the higher price of gold is accompanied by New York inventories surging this year.

As a result of tariff fears, an arbitrage opportunity emerged for major players like JPMorgan Chase (JPM) and HSBC (HSBC); the price spread between London Bullion and Comex widened after Trump announced his intention to introduce tariffs on aluminum and steel imports. According to Comex filings, JPMorgan is going to deliver $4 billion of gold in February, reflecting the impact higher demand for gold has right now on the momentum.

As long as the tariff risks are not resolved, gold should remain one of the most attractive assets among traders and investors. Yahoo Finance UK readers were asked about their expectations and 60% believed that gold could reach $3,500 in 2025.

The Case for Gold Now

So, the question for any investor who missed the action right now is how those tariffs are going to play out. First of all, you have to understand that the effects of tariffs on inflation are debated. The honest answer is that we just don't know how this will play out (but there's good reason to believe that the uncertainty will drag out; more in a bit). One Fed official believes that the tariffs will not result in much higher prices and that the impact on inflation will not persist. Others prefer to err on the side of caution and assume that the effects will last longer.

Economic growth may also take a hit; The Tax Foundation projects that the Canada/Mexico tariffs could decrease GDP growth by as much as 0.3% while tariffs on China could reduce it by 0.1%. While it estimates that steel/aluminum tariffs will have a much more modest impact (less than a 0.05% decrease), we still don't know how the so-called reciprocal tariffs scheduled to take effect in April will play out.

At the same time, the tariffs could introduce trade tensions among the U.S. and other countries. I believe that this is the primary reason for investors rushing to buy gold than the current forecasts on economic growth that rely on things we do know about. After all, a trade war could make things much worse. I think that the market correctly assesses that retaliation is certain if Trump goes forward with his plans regarding tariffs. China's more lukewarm response may have something to do with the fact that the Trump administration "discriminated" against Canada and Mexico by announcing a 10% tariff on imports from China, rather than the more aggressive 25% for the two neighboring countries. Canada and Mexico retaliated in kind before they came to an agreement, but the tariffs were simply postponed. At the same time, the EU Commission has stated that it would react "firmly and immediately" against any tariff increases the Trump administration announces.

All of this fuels the uncertainty I mentioned, and as long as this "tariff" matter is present, the markets have no reason to change course. Which brings me to the fact that tariffs on Canada and Mexico have been postponed until March 4. That's less than two weeks from now. However, reciprocal tariffs may be coming as early as April 2. The gold price action is likely to have more room to continue its strong trend upward in the short term.

Technicals

Speaking of which, the technical picture both agrees with the fundamentals and shows us promising signs of an enduring trend. The price action has widened the distance between the current level and the 50-day moving average, indicating a strong demand; it's interesting to see how the 50-day moving average has played the role of a soft resistance level in the last months of 2024, but the pattern was broken last month.

As indicated by the Bollinger Band channel widening, volatility is rising, which is to be expected with such a strong trend. At the same time, the current price being that close to the upper band reflects the strong momentum.

Now, pay attention to this. The bands are set at 2.5 standard deviations, so only 1.2% of the price closes should occur outside the bands, making the multiple times that the price closed above the upper band lately a rare phenomenon. This also represents the very strong demand for gold right now.

As for the RSI being just above 70 right now, many traders may think that it means gold is overbought. Within the context of the fundamentals, it actually indicates high momentum. The RSI doesn't have much predictive value for trend reversals, in my opinion. I think it's more reasonable to use it to simply assess the momentum because if you examine the chart above, the RSI has managed to cross 70 and stay meaningfully above it for some time. Last week it went down, below 70, and now it's back above that level again.

Risks

So, the primary risks come down to fundamentals. Because gold is something that I believe all diversified portfolios should have to some extent as a hedge against market volatility and economic uncertainty such as today, I will have to talk about opportunity risks. The long-term case for gold is after all strong but it was beyond the scope of this article.

First of all, since there's good reason to believe that the current price action is more of a result of trade policy uncertainty rather than monetary policy because of the magnitude of the price increase, the timeline, and how other assets have behaved, you should keep an eye on that factor.

Trump is relatively unpredictable as president and I have my reservations regarding the extent to which tariff threats are more bark than bite; put it another way, some policies have the end goal of a non-economic outcome but others (like reciprocal tariffs) may be more bite than bark. The markets price in the uncertainty but should price it out if said uncertainty is not replaced with actions as bold as the "threats" in regard to tariffs. Maybe the tariffs on Canadian and Mexican imports will be "postponed indefinitely" next month. Maybe reciprocal tariffs will not ignite a trade war and instead manage to bring tariffs on U.S. exports down. Maybe tariffs on aluminum and steel don't have to be followed by tariffs on gold (which is probably a key factor when it comes to the price action right now).

Moreover, because there is a connection between tariffs and a higher risk of higher inflation, there is a good reason to believe that essentially the market is concerned about higher inflation as a result of tariffs. So, bear in mind that more positive inflation expectations as a result of data that prove the current uptrend of CPI to be less threatening could result in a pullback that would be independent of the potential impact of tariffs.

All of these factors could change the current trend of gold outperforming other assets, and a tilt to gold could result in an opportunity cost.

These are all matters you should monitor, and I will do my best to keep up with the important changes regarding the outlook for gold and write relevant articles in the coming weeks.

For now, I look forward to your comments and having a discussion about the outlook for gold with you. By the way, I have no problem talking about politics but I think that such a discussion should be in the context of trading and investing.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: Foreign Direct Investment Increased To A Record $41 Trillion
data.description:
Global foreign direct investment grew again in 2023 after declining the previous year. Inward direct investment climbed $1.75 trillion, or 4.4 percent, reaching a record $41 trillion, according to the IMF's latest Coordinated Direct Investment Survey, which provides detailed information on direct investment positions between countries.

FDI rose in most regions, with Central and South Asia, Europe, and North and Central America contributing most. Direct investment between advanced economies grew by $880 billion, or 3.6 percent, while those from advanced economies to emerging market and developing economies rose by $538 billion, or 7.6 percent.

As our Chart of the Week shows, the United States extended its lead as the top destination for direct investment. Singapore recorded the largest gain in 2023, with its position rising $307 billion, followed by $227 billion for the United States and $164 billion for Germany. Meanwhile, the Netherlands and Luxembourg posted the steepest declines but remained in the top five, alongside the United States, China, and the United Kingdom.

The United States continues to lead as the top destination for inward foreign direct investment

Top 10 FDI recipients, billions of USD

Strong growth was also seen in many emerging economies. Most notably, India, Mexico, and Brazil each saw their inward direct investment positions rise by around $130 billion or about 20 percent, marking the largest increase for these three economies in total since the survey began in 2009.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: EQT: Lower Costs Delivered
data.description:
One of the prime reasons that EQT (NYSE:EQT) gave for its latest acquisition was the ability to lower costs. It would appear that those lower costs were delivered as stated. The last article discussed expectations on these lower costs. It would appear that those expectations are being met and will last well into the future. The anticipated coming recovery of natural gas prices (besides what happened with this cold winter) will be "icing on the cake".

The savings came primarily from the gathering costs due to the acquisition of Equitrans Midstream in July 2024. Transmission costs did increase due to the startup of the Mountain Valley pipeline. But management will likely set a goal for some add-on projects to at least partially bring those costs back down.

The very cold winter is likely to provide a significant boost to first quarter profits from rising natural gas prices. Longer term, there is a very good possibility of natural gas prices joining the world market, which has far stronger prices than the usually oversupplied North American market. The future outlook for a natural gas company like this one could hardly be better.

Cash Flow

Cash flow was surprisingly strong in the fourth quarter, despite the perception of weak natural gas prices.

The average realized price was $3.01 per MCF, which was higher than the previous year. Now it is not all that unusual for rising prices to require more working capital as those prices rise. In this case, other assets and liabilities did use a lot of cash in the fourth quarter as shown above.

For the fiscal year, 2023 received a benefit from working capital changes. The ability to self-finance working capital needs without additional borrowing is often a requirement for rating the debt.

Despite a challenging fiscal year, adjusted operating cash flow improved somewhat from the year before. EQT could well get a boost from both the acquisition and rising commodity prices to produce a "barn burner" first quarter. The effects could well flow through to the cash flow statement as well.

Efficiency Gains

Well efficiency gains have accelerated for Marcellus operators. This has led to some industry-leading percentage cost gains over the past year.

Efficiency gains tend to come in lumps. It should therefore be no surprise that the gains in store for the current fiscal year are quite a bit less than what happened in fiscal year 2024. It seems to be typical in this industry to make minimal efficiency gains for a few years, and then one big year comes along. Over time, these gains keep the commodity prices from advancing at the rate of inflation. Commodity prices do rise over time. But they usually rise a lot slower than inflation.

One of the reasons for the increasing relative performance has to be at least some of the acquisitions made since the Rice Brothers won the proxy battle to run the company.

What you are seeing is not only are costs coming down, but well performance is exceeding earlier relative performance compared to peers. This has led to a rise in the fiscal 2025 guidance that was mentioned by management as part of the fourth quarter earnings press release.

In the commodity business, every little bit saved often has a significant effect on the bottom line because the commodity business is often known as "a game of pennies". It does not take too many additional pennies saved per sales dollar to begin to materially outperform the industry.

Sales Prices Are Firming

The Marcellus Basin was long oversupplied with takeaway transportation issues. This company has long sought transportation to stronger priced markets. However, now help has arrived in the form of lower discounts to benchmark prices.

Clearly the startup of the Mountain Valley Pipeline has gotten some of that over-supply out of the basin. That alone will strengthen prices. But there is some expected demand growth as shown above that could improve the situation still more.

This could mean that Marcellus Basin producers might (overall) outperform the industry in the future. This would primarily favor those that never got the natural gas out of the basin to stronger markets. But all basin producers benefit from stronger prices and less oversupply.

Balance Sheet

Management has aggressively tackled the debt balance ever since the acquisition closed for Equitrans Midstream.

The emphasis here was always on the advantages of investment grade. Therefore, management has wasted no time in getting debt levels to a comfortable investment grade level that provides financial flexibility if another attractive deal presents itself.

This is yet another company that is expecting natural gas prices to be better. As noted above, the deleveraging strategy largely eliminated any financial risk that a lack of hedging may cause.

The cold winter may hasten the natural gas pricing recovery prospects because it is decreasing storage levels faster than many anticipated. The other potential bright spot for the industry is that La Nina summers typically are hotter than normal which could again imply a period of higher-than-expected demand for natural gas. Right now, things look very good for the industry.

Summary

The long-term acquisition strategy that included both acreage and midstream capacity has allowed the company to quickly diversify its sales efforts.

It was not that many years ago when EQT dumped its production into the oversupplied Marcellus sales market. That caused a fairly large pricing discount from any suitable benchmark. The acquisition strategy changed that.

Acquisitions also garnered acreage that has allowed for well outperformance in the Marcellus basin. As a result, this is a very different company than it was five years ago.

Long-time readers may remember that this management took over the reins of the company after a proxy battle not long before covid challenges presented themselves. This management had to move fast to get this company through the covid challenges. To the benefit of shareholders, this management has continually improved company prospects.

The improvement reiterates the strong buy consideration of this idea. Any management that can do what was accomplished here in terms of outperformance (as well as performance improvement) is unlikely to slow down any time soon. A better management would be hard to find.

Personally, I would likely hold a company like this until the story changes or the stock becomes ridiculously overpriced. Growth stories like this are not that common in the commodity business.

Risks

Future commodity prices are both low visibility and very volatile. The anticipated natural gas price recovery could be delayed or even not happen for reasons that are not currently apparent. A severe and sustained commodity price downturn could materially change the company outlook.

The natural gas demand foreseen by the increasing data centers that need a reliable electric supply may not occur if another less costly reliable method is found. It likely would take a pretty good technology leap for that to happen. But stranger things have happened in this industry.

A loss of key personnel could materially set back the future of the company.

A continuing risk is the maintenance of the competitive place that the company has in the industry. Any company could fall behind in both pricing competitiveness and cost competitiveness. Things could go favorably as well. So far, this company has materially improved its competitive position over the years. But there is no certainty that the improvement will be maintained or will grow.

data.title: Navitas Semiconductor Q4 Earnings Preview: Powers Up For A Pivotal Quarter
data.description:
Thesis

Navitas Semiconductor (NASDAQ:NVTS), a company that set up shop in 2014 in sunny California, has made it its mission to create power chips that are quietly revolutionizing the way our gadgets, cars, and various electronic contraptions function. Essentially, they work with gallium nitride (GAN) and silicon carbide (SiC), which allow devices to charge more quickly, waste less energy, and stick around for longer before they conk out. These clever little components find their way into all sorts of places, from smartphones and sprawling data centers to electric vehicles and solar panels. Perhaps most notably, their GaNFast chips make fast charging not just possible but also much more efficient, while their GeneSiC devices, which are designed with longevity and reliability in mind, run at lower temperatures and, if all goes to plan, outlast the conventional alternatives by a considerable margin.

If you were feeling particularly brave about jumping into this struggling stock, mid-November 2024 would have been your moment. Right after its Q3 earnings, when things were looking bleak, the stock began to show signs of life and started picking up positive momentum. In fact, over the last five days alone, it managed to climb 17%, which, given its rough 1 year performance, could turn out to be either a promising turnaround or just a brief moment of optimism before reality set back in.

With Q4 earnings set to release on February 24, 2025, after the market closes, Navitas is gearing up for a busy few weeks. Once the numbers are out and the earnings call is wrapped up, the company will hit the investor circuit, making appearances at three major events: the Susquehanna 14th Annual Technology Conference (February 28, virtual), the Morgan Stanley Technology, Media & Telecom Conference (March 3, San Francisco), and the 37th Annual Roth Conference (March 17, Dana Point).

CFO Todd Glickman will be making the rounds at all three events, while CEO Gene Sheridan will join in for the Morgan Stanley conference, where he’ll take part in a "fireside chat" at 2:35 p.m. Pacific. With all this in motion, it seems like as good a time as any to take a step back, dig through the fundamentals, and try to determine whether this is a smart moment to invest or just another case of misplaced optimism.

The Case for Optimism

Navitas Semiconductor is staking its claim in the most promising frontiers of the 2020s tech ecosystem, and its latest strategic moves reflect a deep understanding of the industry’s shifting energy demands. The company’s Q3 announcement of its low-voltage GaN technology for AI data centers and next-generation EVs is a timely response to a growing crisis: energy consumption. With AI workloads projected to devour up to 4% of global electricity by 2030, according to Arm Holdings, hyperscalers like AWS, Azure, and Google are under mounting pressure to improve power efficiency.

And so, we arrive at Navitas’ new 48V GaN platform, built specifically for data centers and aimed squarely at tackling this growing problem. The company, for its part, seems to be making decent headway, with 60 active customer projects in enterprise power solutions already underway. On top of that, it has teamed up with Delta and LiteON, two companies in power infrastructure, which certainly doesn’t hurt its chances of gaining a stronger foothold in the AI energy efficiency race.

The financial incentives are compelling. Every watt saved through GaN’s superior efficiency translates into millions in operational savings for cloud providers, positioning Navitas as one of the few semiconductor companies directly linked to the AI infrastructure boom. Data centers already account for about 2%+ of the world’s electricity use, a figure that, thanks to AI, is only going to head in one direction: up. Understandably, the big cloud providers are now in something of a hurry to make their operations more energy efficient. And it turns out that even the smallest improvements can make a surprisingly big difference. Texas Instruments, for instance, estimates that a modest 0.8% boost in efficiency at a 100-megawatt data center using GaN-based systems could save around $7 million in energy costs over ten years. Likewise, GaN Systems estimates that upgrading power supply units (PSUS) with GaN could generate an additional $3 million in profit per ten server racks while slashing over 100 metric tons of CO2 emissions annually.

Beyond data centers, Navitas is making inroads in another high-growth sector: electric vehicles. Its six new design wins, while frustratingly backloaded to 2025-2026 production, signal a major shift, automakers are moving beyond experimental SiC trials and are now seriously qualifying GaN for onboard systems. The AEC-Q101 qualification of Navitas’ GeneSiC devices, the gold standard for automotive electronics, proves that Navitas has put in the rigorous engineering hours to meet the industry’s unforgiving zero-defect standards.

This traction comes at a pivotal moment for the EV market. When you look back at Tesla's (TSLA) Q3 margins which rebounded to 19.8%, it reinforced the idea that EV profitability is achievable at scale. The company’s ability to drive down cost per vehicle to $35,100 demonstrates how economies of scale, coupled with advanced power electronics, are unlocking the next phase of cost-efficient EV production.

Navitas stands to benefit, particularly in China, the world’s largest EV market, where it derives over 70% of its revenue. Both domestic and global automakers are under intense pressure to optimize powertrain costs, and Navitas’ acquisition of GeneSiC expands its portfolio to deliver next-generation GaN and SiC solutions in a $20 billion annual market.

Arguably the most overlooked but quietly brilliant move in Navitas’ long-term game plan is its partnership with Infineon. In theory, by getting GaN specifications in line for dual sourcing, Navitas is not only reducing the risk of supply chain meltdowns (always a good idea) but also setting the stage for what could be a full-scale industry shake-up. And, if history is anything to go by, this sort of thing tends to work out quite well, just ask the folks who once thought memory chips and LEDs were niche before standardization turned them into everyday necessities. Now, the same playbook is being dusted off for GaN, and with a little luck, it might just work again.

With revenues amounting to EUR3.424 billion in Q1 2025, Infineon (OTCQX:IFNNY)isn’t exactly a lightweight in the semiconductor world, and its backing gives this whole GaN initiative a lot more oomph. In fact, just by existing in the same room as Infineon, GaN gets a much better shot at being taken seriously as a proper alternative to silicon carbide in industrial and automotive circles. And should this partnership actually manage to convince the industry to standardize around GaN, well, that could fast-track a $20 billion-plus shift in high-power applications.

Meanwhile, Navitas’ recent 26 mobile charger design wins with Tier-1 OEMs, though lower-margin, serve as a strategic foothold. These products act as a “gateway drug” for GaN adoption, familiarizing both consumers and engineers with the technology’s advantages before expanding into more complex, high-stakes applications.

Valuation & Growth Potential

On paper, the current reality for Navitas is that it sports a negative P/E ratio of -14.15x, which is another way of saying “not profitable.”

Analysts, however, seem to believe that by 2027, all of this will change dramatically, so much so that Navitas is projected to have a 229x P/E. This, presumably, reflects an unshakable faith that the company will go from burning through cash to an actual profitable company in just three years. But, of course, that raises an inconvenient question: is any of this remotely realistic? For a semiconductor firm still in the thick of its spend-now-earn-later phase, this kind of valuation suggests that even the slightest misstep, whether in revenue growth, margin expansion, or customer adoption, could send things (further) south.

For now, profitability remains something of a mirage, always just a little farther down the road. The one-year forward EPS estimate of -$0.29 is, technically speaking, an improvement over last year’s -$0.31, though not the sort that inspires much celebration. Break-even, it seems, is still very much a work in progress. Even analysts appear hesitant, building in a 10% margin of error for 2025 and a more anxious 20% for 2026, which is our industry's inventive way of saying that no one is entirely sure how this will play out.

The models optimistically predict an earnings turning point somewhere between 2025 and 2026, but for that to happen, Navitas will need to ramp up revenue in a big way and, crucially, start making money at a rate that actually justifies its current sky-high valuation.

One financial bright spot, however, is Navitas’ strong liquidity position. With $98.61 million in cash and just $7.86 million in total debt, the company enjoys a level of flexibility that many early-stage semiconductor firms lack. This net cash position provides a crucial runway to continue investing in R&D, scaling operations, and expanding its customer base without immediate pressure to raise capital. That said, this cushion is not unlimited. Q3 GAAP operating losses reached $29 million, and if gross margins fail to improve in the near future, Navitas may eventually need to seek additional funding.

Risks & Considerations

In the short term, things are looking a little rocky. Recent financial results have made it abundantly clear that revenue isn’t exactly booming, and margins aren’t doing much better. In Q3, Navitas brought in $21.7 million, which, aside from being a 1.35% drop from the previous year, also came up $253,980 short of expectations, an amount that, while not catastrophic, is certainly enough to be irritating.

Unfortunately, Q4 doesn’t promise much improvement, with projected revenue falling somewhere between $18 million and $20 million, meaning yet another decline. The main issue? Delays in key EV and industrial power projects. Two particularly important ones that were supposed to give Q4 a much-needed boost have now been unceremoniously kicked down the road to Q2 and Q3 of 2025. So, whatever revenue lift they were supposed to provide won’t be happening anytime soon, which, in turn, stretches out Navitas’ already uphill climb toward profitability.

Meanwhile, gross margins aren’t exactly covering themselves in glory. In Q3 2023, they were a respectable 42.1%, but by Q3 2024, they had shuffled downward to 40.1%, which is the kind of trend that does not inspire much enthusiasm. And, if forecasts are to be believed, Q4 isn’t likely to offer much of a turnaround, with margins expected to hover somewhere around 40%, give or take 50 basis points. The main culprit here is the company’s increasing reliance on mobile chargers, perfectly fine as a revenue stream, but unfortunately not nearly as profitable as the industrial and EV applications Navitas would probably prefer to be selling more of.

Management insists that margins will improve once higher-power segments, like AI data centers and EV powertrains, start to ramp up. Exactly when that will happen, however, remains anyone’s guess. Adding to the fun, competitors in China and Europe, sitting on underutilized fabs and presumably not thrilled about it, might decide to start aggressive pricing. If that happens, Navitas will find itself in an unenviable position: either slash margins to stay in the game or hold the line on pricing and watch customers wander off to cheaper alternatives.

Beyond the more immediate financial challenges, there’s also the not-so-small issue of actually getting industries to adopt GaN. High-voltage GaN tech, while definitely promising, is facing a long, somewhat frustrating road in sectors that have already settled quite comfortably into using silicon carbide. In theory, GaN should be the better choice in some applications, both in terms of cost and performance. But, as CEO Gene Sheridan has admitted himself, getting to the point where it’s commercially viable isn’t something that’s going to happen overnight. More like years, not quarters.

The Verdict: Buy

Looking ahead, the Q4 report and investor conferences present both opportunity and peril. Positive surprises could come from a forward guidance beat, so if Q1 2025 revenue guidance exceeds, thus breaking the seasonal slump pattern, it would signal design wins converting faster than expected and pipeline updates, any increase beyond the $1.6B design pipeline (especially in AI/EVs) would validate a TAM expansion thesis.

After considering all of this, here’s my takeaway: Navitas Semiconductor is absolutely not for the faint of heart. But for those with a 3-5 year investment horizon and a reasonably high tolerance for volatility, it does offer a certain lopsided potential, one of those situations where the upside could be much larger than the downside, provided things go even vaguely to plan.

The upcoming Morgan Stanley talk on March 3rd could be one defining moment. If CEO Gene Sheridan manages to present a clear, believable roadmap to hitting $100 million in quarterly revenue by 2026, that could get investors excited. Needham, for what it’s worth, is even more enthusiastic, pegging 2026 sales at $140 million. If that happens, the stock could find itself heading toward the $6-$8 range. On the other hand, if Sheridan delivers the kind of vague, hedging commentary that suggests timelines are slipping or margin pressure is worse than expected, NVTS could find itself right back down at the $2.50 support level.

Right now, Navitas feels like Qualcomm in 2004, ahead of the smartphone boom but burning cash. For my money, I’d take a starter position here, but keep powder dry for potential dips post-Q4. The knife has fallen, but there’s a handle forming.

data.title: Japan Inflation Spikes To 4.0%, Much Worse Than In The U.S.; BOJ Raises Rate To Just 0.5%
data.description:
The "all items" Consumer Price Index for Japan surged by 4.02% in January year-over-year, the worst inflation rate since January 2023, and beyond that one month, the worst rate since 1981 (red in the chart).

The all-items CPI minus "imputed rent" - a calculated figure derived from other indicators - spiked to 4.6%, according to Statistics Japan on Friday (blue).

Fresh food prices soared by 7.8% year-over-year, driven by prices of fresh goods, +22%, including +25.6% for vegetables and seaweeds and +22.6% for fresh fruits. Inflation in fresh foods has been bad since early 2022.

Japanese households devote a relatively large share of their spending to food, a far higher share than any other country in the G7, based on the Engel coefficient, according to the Nikkei last November. So this hurts:

Energy prices soared by 10.8% year-over-year, with electricity soaring by 18.1% and gas to the home up by 6.2%:

This includes electricity, manufactured & piped gas, liquefied propane, kerosene (used in common household space heaters), and gasoline.

Core CPI - the all-items CPI minus fresh food - accelerated to 3.2%, the worst since June 2023.

Excluding both fresh food and energy, inflation rose by 2.6%. This is a key metric for the Bank of Japan's 2% inflation target.

The Bank of Japan has raised its policy rate three times to a whopping 0.5%, starting from -0.1%. And it indicated that it would raise rates further in baby steps. The BOJ also started QT in 2024 and is accelerating it in 2025, but it's a slow process, and its balance sheet is gigantic.

With the BOJ's policy rate at 0.5% and the 10-year yield of Japanese Government Bonds (JGBs) at 1.34% currently, investors in fixed income products are getting eaten alive on a daily basis by this 4.0% inflation.

By contrast, in the US, CPI is at 3.0% (Japan is at 4.0%), while the midpoint of the Fed's policy rates is 4.33%, and the 10-year Treasury yield is at 4.5%, still compensating investors for inflation plus some.

But Japan's long-run fiscal mess, with a debt-to-GDP ratio of 254% in 2024, is more than twice as terrible as the US fiscal mess with a debt-to GDP ratio of 122%.

The Bank of Japan is clearly trying to resolve Japan's sovereign debt problem, the biggest in the developed world, by fueling inflation to devalue that debt - along with everything else denominated in yen.

And it's working in its insidious way: Under the impact of this inflation, the debt-to-GDP ratio has been edging lower, from the peak of 260% in 2022. Turns out, there are no free lunches after all, someone is going to pay for it, and it's the Japanese households, through much higher prices and steeply negative "real" yields on their yield investments. But after decades of reckless government profligacy, there really aren't many alternatives left to deal with it.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Discovery Silver: Buying At The Top Of The Market, Maybe
data.description:
It is possible here that Discovery Silver (OTCQX:DSVSF) has just bought a prime asset in a rising market. It's also possible that Discovery has bought a middling asset at the top of the market. Your view - and mine - about the future prospects for Discovery are going to depend upon which of the two you - or I - think it has just done.

It's not cut and dried either way. There are good arguments in favour of their current deal. There are also those arguments against it as well. So, a view of the stock will depend upon the balancing of those two sets of arguments. I come away thinking that the balance is not favourable. Not as an absolute, but on a balance of probabilities basis. The view is therefore just to pass and do something else.

The past

Up until last month, Discovery was a silver play. The Cordero Project, apparently the world's largest undeveloped silver deposit. I'm not wholly sure that's true. I would believe the largest known but as yet undeveloped, but there's an awful lot of the world as yet unexplored in any detail. But, OK. There's a deck here.

The latest results are nothing very much out of the ordinary for a company in that situation:

They're spending their cash to try to develop the project. They've a feasibility study which shows a positive NPV ("NPV5% of US$1.2 billion at US$22 per ounce silver" and so on) and, well, it's the standard mining development bet at this point.

Are the management up to bringing the mine into production? What are costs and the silver price going to do? As so often, what's the political environment (ie, is Mexico a place where they'll be able to keep the mine?) and so on.

I'm not a silver analyst, so this isn't something for me to thumbs up or down.

On the basic mining numbers, they've cash to keep developing for another year before they need a big infusion of money. That rising share count I'd expect to include some - but not enough to finance fully - sales at the market. It's all rather normal for a company at this stage of the development game.

It's also not really important for there's been that transformational deal at the company.

Newmont and gold

The company changed, radically, at the end of January:

On January 27, 2025, Discovery announced that it had entered into a definitive agreement to acquire from a wholly owned subsidiary of Newmont Corporation, 100% ownership in Newmont’s Porcupine Complex, based in and near Timmins, Ontario, Canada.

This is a combination here. There are some resources (so, deposits but which are not, as yet, proven to be economic but shown to be likely to be economic), actual mines and ongoing production.

The presentation is here.

Discovery's valuation depends upon this gold

There's been a significant jump - 100% or so, around and about - since even the thought of this deal became known. So, the market is thinking that this is truly transformational on the upside.

It certainly is transformational as well. Going from being a silver developer with a feasibility study to owning actual gold production is indeed transformative.

At which point this isn't, not really, about the gold mines themselves. It's about two and only two things. One is the claimed value add here, which I agree exists. The other is are they buying at the top of the market? That's an unknown, but the strategy worries me.

Financing the buy

If they had been able to buy the gold simply for paper - for stock - then I'd say this is a lovely deal. All we'd have to worry about then would be the gold price - as with every other gold miner. Sadly, they haven't. They've paid cash in part. And possibly more than you might think, too.

Financing for the deal looks like it's in two parts, but it's really in three. The two parts are paying paper - good - and paying cash. Paying cash isn't necessarily bad but it's the bit I worry about.

The upfront Discovery shares, that's just paying paper for paper. That's fine by me. The life of mine royalty - or the raising of cash by selling that - is, in my description here, just paper again. It's not quite equity, and it's not quite a preference share because it's a royalty. But in other ways it's quite like either of those. If there's no production then there's no payment necessary. If the gold price falls (or rises, obviously) then the royalty changes. It is swapping paper for the cash that closes the deal.

The bought deal, well, that's already happened and so it's not our cash in it.

However, the deferred cash is a real cash owed number. So too is the senior debt facility. That's real cash that has to be repaid no matter what. And, here's the thing, so too is that repayable NSR royalty. As in the name, it's repayable. Its debt secured against the run of production, but it's still debt.

That's the bit that worries me.

Yes, sure, there's production there. They're now in gold where they weren't before. But they've also got significant debt which really does have to be paid back.

And, well. "Base Case NPV of $1.2B1,2 or $2.3B using $2,650/oz LT gold price, with growth potential" That's the bit I worry about.

Yes, those are reasonable enough gold prices for today (the $2,650 is a 23% uplift on the base case). But here's the thing. Yes, gold is up. But gold's been on a tear recently. With the debt used to buy in that makes Discovery vulnerable to any significant reversal in that gold price. And the idea that gold is going to stay up there makes me nervous.

The all in sustainable cost here is $1,504 an ounce, as they say. That's not a low-cost base. Add in that debt load that must be repaid - over and above working capital - and we don't need that much of a reversal to something at or under long-term averages to start making things a bit sweaty.

We are, after all, seeing some things that might make us think the gold price is a bit "toppy" at present. There's so much New York futures action that the prices of physical gold, London/New York, have diverged enough to see stock moving across the Atlantic. Sure, OK, that's just arbitrage over the $5 to $20 an ounce price difference but such gaps only do open out in the more frantic times in the gold market.

We'll come back to this.

On the plus side

It's also true that Discovery has been able to buy into a well known gold province at what are - given the current gold price - reasonable looking prices. It's well known that Newmont has been looking to unload so they can concentrate on their own core assets:

“Today’s announcement represents a significant milestone for Newmont as we have agreed to sell the final non-core operation from our divestiture program. The sale is part of Newmont’s ongoing program to divest non-core assets as we make a strategic shift to focus on our Tier 1 assets,” said Tom Palmer, Newmont’s President and Chief Executive Officer.

And, well, yes. So, that's proof that this is not a Tier 1 asset, obviously enough. So, maybe it's not all that good an asset. On the other hand, large companies often do have perfectly decent, but smaller assets left unregarded. You know, the stepchild thing. So, perhaps management that can focus open - and is incentivised by - that smaller project can do better?

And the people running Discovery and these gold operations are all people with significant knowledge of operating in the Timmins Camp, if not actually from the areas originally.

So, to be colloquial about it, perhaps the unloved ginger stepchild really can turn into the success with a bit of attention. It was Ron Weasley who got Hermione after all. Sorry, sorry, promise that's the only Harry Potter reference.

That does make sense to me. As a possibility at least. Local experts, freed from the central corporate bureaucracy, can make a go of second tier assets. As a concept, I'm fine with that.

On balance

The price being paid here looks reasonable enough given the current gold price. The idea that local experts can improve performance is fine by me.

But. There's what I call the John Paul Getty Problem here.

That man's grand fortune was made in oil, as we know. But the basic technique was that sometimes it's cheaper to go drilling for oil on the stock exchange floor. The point being that when the price of the commodity is high then extant assets are highly priced. At which point it's better to go searching for unknown, currently undiscovered, assets. But when the commodity price is low then it's cheaper to buy extant, producing, assets. Markets have that terrible tendency to overshoot after all.

As with, as we've seen here, Altura Mining which went bust when the lithium price was low and then made a fortune for Pilbara (OTCPK:PILBF). Buying extant mineral assets *before* the price rise makes fortunes. But, equally, buying mineral assets after the price rise can make large losses. As I suspect a number of lithium miners are going to find out if they haven't already. For prices have that habit of reverting.

And that's the issue which tips the balance for me. The same Porcupine deal - at the appropriate price of course - for Discovery before the gold price run up would be a buy in from me. When the gold price is up near 50% from a year ago, well, maybe not.

Why I might be wrong

There are two reasons why I might be wrong. One is simply that the effect of locally knowledgeable management upon operations is going to be so great that my concerns about the price paid are irrelevant. OK, could happen.

The other is that the gold price is not just on a tear but has moved to a permanently higher level and is going to keep going. That's also possible.

My opinion

I do think this is a good deal. I also think that at the current 100% up since the announcement this is fully priced - or even more than fully.

I worry about buying assets at the top of the market. I worry about using debt to do so - because debt always has to be repaid even in a falling market.

On balance I think this is one of those where we just go "Oh, that's nice" and instead go and do something else. No doubt others have different opinions.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Case For Indian Telecoms
data.description:
The stars for the second-largest mobile market in the world are now aligned: an oligopolistic market structure, a range of demand drivers, and anticipated tariff increases. Below, we discuss the Indian telecommunications sector's transformation over the last decade, highlight its main growth drivers, and explore the recent regulatory developments in the space.

The Indian telecom sector has transitioned from a fragmented market to an oligopolistic structure dominated by only three players, and is now well positioned to benefit from India's demographic dividend and the monetization of the burgeoning demand for connectivity. Buoyed by substantial investment and favorable economic conditions, the sector's growth is poised to outpace the Asia-Pacific telcos average.

From Fragmentation to Oligopoly

The Indian telecom sector is on a growth path, buoyed by favorable demographics; increasing mobile data consumption; expanding availability of 4G, 5G, and digital services; and supportive industry dynamics. The current growth phase follows an extended period of disruption and consolidation, intensified by regulatory hurdles.

Looking back, in 2016 and 2017 the sector witnessed a sharp decline in average revenue per user (ARPU) following the entrance of Reliance Jio. Fierce price wars followed, forcing incumbents to lower their prices. Consequently, the competitive environment drove many players out of the market and the sector experienced significant consolidation. By 2018, the market had reduced from eight players to an oligopoly dominated by just three operators, as shown in exhibit 1-Reliance Jio, Bharti Airtel, and Vodafone Idea-with the smaller operators exiting or consolidating into larger firms.

Regulatory challenges have marred progress in the sector in the past, putting financial strain on the operators and hindering domestic and international investments. Spectrum prices have also been historically high in India, and a dispute between the government and the operators over adjusted gross revenue (AGR) dues, which was ruled in favor of the government, led to significant financial liabilities for operators across the industry. Although the government alleviated the financial pressure by offering flexible payment options, these struggles led the top two operators with healthier financials to garner more than 70% of the industry's subscribers, as the smaller players particularly struggled with their financial positions.

Now, these major players can enjoy operating in the second-largest telecommunications industry globally by number of subscribers. Based on a rating agency's report[1], the sector's revenue is projected to grow by more than 15% year-over-year in 2024 and by 11% in 2025, outperforming the Asian telcos, which are expected to grow by mid single digits in 2024 and by low single digits in 2025. This environment, therefore, can offer higher revenue visibility, improving free cash flow generation, stronger balance sheets, and overall improved credit metrics.

Additionally, on the back of rising private consumption, the International Monetary Fund (IMF)[2] has revised upward India's real gross domestic product (GDP) growth to 7% in 2024 and 6.5% in 2025. This positions India among the fastest-growing major economies globally, and the country's positive economic backdrop augurs well for the industry's growth outlook.

In the following section, we will explore the key growth drivers supporting the industry outlook.

Exhibit 1 - Subscriber Market Share in Indian's Wireless Market

Growth Drivers

We believe a young population, increases in per-capita income, the rising adoption of mobile technologies, and a more favorable urban/rural distribution will continue broadening the addressable market and drive demand for advanced services such as 5G. These factors, coupled with efforts to connect underserved regions and narrow the digital divide between urban and rural areas, represent structural demand drivers key to the sector's revenue growth.

1. India's Demographic Dividend

We believe demographics will be a key tailwind for the sector. India has a large, young workforce with a median age of 28.4 years, surpassing China as of 2023.

Still, India's success in leveraging its formidable labor pool hinges on effective labor reforms and job creation. Structural issues in its labor market, such as low labor-force participation and high unemployment/underemployment remain areas of focus. These hurdles have created barriers to labor contributing more to India's GDP growth.[3] Specifically, in a survey released in September 2024,[4] the labor-force participation rate in India stood at 60.1%, primarily due to low female participation, which stands at a precarious 41.7%. For comparison, China's female labor participation rate was 60.5% in 2023.

An S&P study[5] suggests that India's demographic dividend will persist at least until 2055. However, growing investments to sustain productivity and GDP growth, job creation, and increased female participation will be key factors in the demand for mobile data consumption and digital solutions.

This environment can enhance an important demographic advantage: younger consumers are more likely to adopt new digital services, which is a catalyst for higher data consumption and spending. This foreshadows a rise in ARPU, directly contributing to mobile operators' revenue growth while incentivizing them to innovate with their digital offerings.

2. Urbanization, Rural Connectivity, and Broadband Boom

India's rising income and low mobile tariffs have contributed to the rising demand for digital services and content. Urbanization and rural connectivity could be the tipping points for greater broadband adoption. The major players will likely leverage these demand factors to enable additional market growth by improving their offerings, particularly in terms of service availability and broadband quality. This involves expanding 4G and 5G networks and fixed broadband through fixed wireless access (FWA).

Ongoing urbanization can boost growth through multiple channels. According to the United Nations (UN), by 2030 more than 50% of India's population will reside in cities, up from the current urbanization rate of about 36%.[6] An urban population is more likely to use data-heavy applications such as cloud services and video streaming, which can drive higher both data consumption and ARPUs.

Consumers in rural regions can also propel growth for telecom operators. Out of India's 1.2 billion wireless and wireline subscribers, 44% are in rural areas, with the penetration numbers suggesting the segment is still largely untapped.[7] India's urban teledensity, as reported by its Telecom Regulatory Authority (TRAI), was 131% in 2024; rural teledensity was far below, at 58%. Digital India and BharatNet, government projects targeted to increase mobile connectivity and connect rural areas through optical fiber, are helping realize the potential of data consumption, improve digital literacy, and bridge the digital divide of underserved rural areas.

On the fixed broadband side, penetration remains low, with only 45 million subscribers[8] as of December 2024. However, a recent study[9] suggests that India's wired broadband subscribers could grow to 70 million by 2026 and reach 100 million by 2030, implying a compound annual growth rate of 14% from 2024 to 2030 (as shown in exhibit 2). This trend is reinforced by the 5G fixed wireless access (FWA) expansion, a strategy that has proven to be more scalable than fiber-to-the-home solutions (FTTH). Admittedly, the increasing home broadband penetration is pertinent to consumer behaviors that demand more connectivity from home and to rising economic growth. Even though the direction of causality[10] between rising economic growth and home broadband penetration remains unclear, the correlation between the two portends well for the industry growth. To that point, the IMF projects that GDP per capita will increase from $2,500 in 2023 to $4,200 by 2029, supporting our view that home broadband penetration should rise alongside.

Interestingly, social media penetration in India is 32% in 2024, much lower than the global average of 63%.[11] Yet, Indians are among the largest data consumers globally. As of March 24, 2024, the average data usage per wireless data subscriber per month is 20.8 gigabytes, which is an increase of more than 17% year-over-year, or 2.3x since December 18, 2023. Studies[12] suggest that average data consumption per user could double by 2027, primarily through increased content and digital services consumption, as shown in exhibit 3.

Exhibit 2 - Wired (Home Broadband) Subscribers (in Millions)

Exhibit 3 - Data Usage Per Subscriber (in Gigabytes)

3. Tariff Recovery

Despite India having the highest data consumption per subscriber, and implementing tariff hikes between 2019 and 2021, ARPUs remain among the lowest in the world. Telecom companies have struggled in the last decade with competition and aggressive price wars, which have kept tariffs low. Before the tariff announcements, monthly ARPU was hovering around $2, which is significantly lower than other selected emerging markets, as shown in exhibits 4 and 5. Investments in network expansion have been heavy, particularly with the rollout of 5G, and future aspirations like 6G, along with spectrum charges and AGR dues, require tariff increases to support the sector's financial sustainability.

The key telecom players have been vocal that a healthier monthly ARPU would be in the range of 200 to 300 rupees in the medium term, higher than the range it hovered in first-quarter 2025 (ended in June 2024) at 145 to 210 rupees per month. With the announcement of a 10% to 25% tariff increase in 2024 and a CAGR of 7% to 13% within the next three years, the target looks achievable. Even though we do expect SIM card consolidation and churn to increase in the next few quarters, the increase in ARPU on the back of the tariff increases would offset any weakness stemming from the subscriber losses. We will monitor the actual ARPU realized; as most of the subscribers are in prepaid plans, it's likely that customers will downgrade to smaller plans or simply recharge less frequently.

We believe another likely ARPU growth driver will be the transition to 4G and 5G plans. Currently, for two out of the three industry players, the percentage of the subscribers not yet on 4G or 5G plans ranges from 25% to 35%. These users generally pay lower tariffs than 4G/5G subscribers because they do not benefit from high-speed internet and premium content. As connectivity expands to rural areas and affordability rises, migrating these subscribers to higher-paying plans presents another area of growth for the key players.

Exhibit 4 - GDP Per Capita Correlation With ARPU

Exhibit 5 - Monthly ARPU by Country

4. Regulatory Shifts and FDI Trends

As mentioned above, the Indian regulatory landscape has exerted considerable influence in the sector, presenting challenges such as spectrum auction prices and the AGR dispute. However, projects such as the Digital India and BharatNet, which promote online infrastructure and digital and financial inclusion, can drive progress. The 2023 Telecommunication Act, enacted in June 2024, aims to update India's telecom legislation by enhancing key areas, such as the simplification of spectrum allocation, cybersecurity, and consumer protection.

A more conducive regulatory backdrop facilitates telecom and infrastructure development, reducing uncertainty, thus incentivizing foreign investors to tap into a particular sector. Looking back, the major players in the sector attracted capital from global investors including BlackRock, Singtel, Qatar Foundation, Facebook, and Google[13], and many private equity firms provided funding for the 4G/5G network expansion and the development of services in the companies' digital ecosystem. These investments were reflected in the foreign direct investment (FDI) data between 2000 and 2024. The telecom sector is among the top four FDI recipient sectors in the country, accounting for 6%[14] of the cumulative FDI inflows.

This trend has decelerated over the past few years, though, due to many factors, as shown in exhibit 6. The consolidation of the sector created a natural barrier of entry, and a less favorable regulatory backdrop might also have dissuaded foreign investors. Since 2021, the government has been raising the FDI limits in the sector, allowing 100% of FDIs through the automatic route without previous regulatory checks to further open up the sector to foreign investors. Additionally, due to the introduction of the 2023 Telecommunication Act, new technology being deployed, and the recent opening up of the satellite communications to the private sector, it wouldn't be a surprise to see FDIs returning to the sector.

Exhibit 6 - India's Telecommunications FDI (in Millions)

Conclusion

We believe the TMT sector in India is at the tipping point of a new era characterized by synchronized demand drivers - including increasing urbanization, rising per capita income, and growing demand for digital services - and complemented by the anticipated tariff increases.

Mobile revenue growth is expected to be driven by improvements in ARPU, while revenue from home broadband is set to accelerate due to a surge in subscriber base supported by improving affordability.

The telecom providers operate in the second-largest telecom industry in the world by number of subscribers. A stable competitive environment, coupled with the aforementioned demand drivers, offers high revenue visibility, better credit metrics, and higher returns.

However, it's key for the telecommunications sector to address challenges, including increasing affordability for low-income segments, continuing to invest in infrastructure, and extending reach to underserved regions.

Key factors for the government to monitor include implementing further labor reforms, maintaining a productive labor pool, creating a conducive regulatory backdrop, and executing digital inclusion policies. Addressing these hurdles is essential for the sector to reach sustainable financial health, attract investments, and create a positive and diverse investment environment that will continue to contribute to the country's economic growth.

Footnotes

[1] Fitch

[2] IMF

[3] IMF

[4] MOSPI

[5] S&P Global

[6] Unhabitat

[7] TRAI

[8] TRAI

[9] EY

[10] IMF

[11] Datareportal

[12] Ericsson

[13] IBEF

[14] DPIIT

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Chart Of The Day: Skepticism Reigns... Despite New SPX Highs
data.description:
By Mike Larson

The S&P 500 just hit an all-time high. Gold just hit (another) all-time high. Stock market selloffs driven by DeepSeek news, fresh Trump Administration tariffs, and other “tape bombs” aren’t lasting long.

Yet, individual investors are the most bearish since November 2023! Or in short, skepticism reigns.

Check out this MoneyShow Chart of the Day. It shows data from the American Association of Individual Investors (AAII) survey. The group has been surveying its members each week for decades to see if they’re bearish or bullish on stocks. And the bear tally just hit its highest in 15 months.

AAII Bearish % Gauge Hits Highest Level in 15 Months

Data by YCharts

Specifically, 47.2% of respondents in the February 13 survey said they were bearish. That was the highest since 50.2% on November 2, 2023. That’s pretty amazing considering the short-term “buy the dip” trading action we’ve seen. Not to mention the fact we just enjoyed two straight years of 20%-plus gains in the S&P 500.

If you attended the 2025 MoneyShow Las Vegas this week, you heard me explain why this is a good thing for markets. But if not, here’s the short version...

When everyone is wildly bullish, even the slightest bit of bad news can fuel aggressive selling. When they’re not, and stocks keep rising anyway, the FOMO effect kicks in on pullbacks. Investors can’t help but buy because they’re afraid they’ll miss the next leg up.

Bottom line: Stay bold!

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Why Coinbase Is Flatlining After Posting Massive Earnings Gains
data.description:
Since the release of its earnings for Fiscal Year (FY) 2024 on the 13th of February through the 19th, the stock price of global cryptocurrency exchange Coinbase Inc (NASDAQ:COIN) has dropped by more than 13%. Going by line item trends, this flies in the face of overall trends.

Trend Drilldown

Net revenues in FY 2024 saw a solid 115% increase in year-on-year (YoY) terms, along with some interesting line item trends being confirmed.

Stablecoins see a retreat in revenue contributions, leading to a 11% reduction in the contribution of "Subscription and Services" to net revenue. "Transactions", meanwhile, saw a staggering 162% increase in revenues being contributed in early signs of 2022's highs hovering into sight for this segment.

Despite the drop in stablecoins' contribution, this category grew 31%. However, blockchain rewards and custodial fee revenues grew to well over twice past years' numbers - a trend that underlines the fact that the crypto space continues to grow unabated. "Transactions" have climbed out of a deep slowdown to register a powerful 162% growth over last year.

At $22.8 billion, overall net income showed an astonishing 2,618% growth over past year's $0.1 billion. Increased transactions and net revenue growth weren't the only tailwinds for this; lowering expenditure played a part too.

While transaction and sales expenses remained fixed, the company's massive expenditure on technology nearly halved, leading to total operating expenses also halving. Overall, net income grew from 3% share of net revenue in FY 2023 to nearly half in FY 2024.

In the "Transactions" segment, consumers (i.e. not institutions) went from generating a little under half of net revenue in FY 2023 to a little over half at 55%. Institutions, meanwhile, remained more-or-less steady at 5% contribution.

The significance of retail investors in Coinbase's transforming fortunes isn't a lesson missed by other players in the market. Competition has been growing by leaps and bounds.

A Competitor and Bitcoin Correlations

Of particular interest amidst a plethora of alternatives in the crowded crypto access business is Robinhood Markets Inc (HOOD), which announced the acquisition of crypto exchange Bitstamp in June last year. Bitstamp is the world's longest-running cryptocurrency exchange and offers crypto trading all over the world—much like Coinbase—and is expected to be completed by the first half (H1) of 2025.

In its quarterly earnings release one day before that of Coinbase, Robinhood declared that over half of its $672 million of transaction-based revenue—$358 million—was crypto-based, marking an over 700% jump from the same quarter last year. The quarter's revenue of $1.01 billion handily beat analysts' consensus of $946 million, while reported earnings per share of $1.01 was more than double that of consensus estimates.

In the new year, the market has already drawn correlations for both stocks with the performance of Bitcoin (BTC-USD).

Of the two stocks, Robinhood is arguably better-diversified as it offers equities and other market instruments on its platform to its U.S. customers. However, in the crypto space, its sights are set to compete with Coinbase globally.

Robinhood's adhesion among retail investors with small portfolios and an interest in established cryptocurrencies hasn't been entirely missed by the market. For retail investors, Robinhood's markups and interest earnings on balances can often work out to be substantially cheaper than the costs implied by the complex fee model Coinbase operates with.

However, both stocks broke their winning streak in the year till date starting from about a week ago, with President Trump's tariff struggles intensifying. As the article describing the effects of President Trump's tariff strategy outlined: while it is entirely possible that sustained tariffs might eventually promote domestic manufacturing, it will remain a pain point for U.S. citizens and businesses alike in the interim. One interesting proxy barometer for outlook on consumption, interestingly, is Walmart's sales outlook due to its long-standing status as a darling of the value-seeking, cost-conscious American consumer.

Walmart's relatively gloomy outlook for 2026 indicated that the retail giant expects sales to be lower, while the current fiscal year's sales to grow by 3-4%, which, when adjusted for real inflationary effects, could constitute a net decrease in total unit sales. The entire market, including recession favorites such as banks, dipped as a result on the 19th of February.

In Conclusion

If there's less money to be spent on supplies, there's bound to be less money available for investments. Given that, both Coinbase and Robinhood have had a precipitous decline over the past week despite Bitcoin rising since the 18th of February which hitherto had also led to these stocks rising.

As Robinhood's Bitstamp acquisition completes and the two platforms are integrated, across-the-board usability will likely increase via the introduction of Bitstamp's advanced trading features to Robinhood's user base. If the battle for crypto dominance is framed as being between Coinbase and Robinhood, the completion of Robinhood's Bitstamp might be the impetus needed for Coinbase to begin rationalizing its fee structure and broadening its investor accessibility.

On the basis of this, the current price depression might be an opportune moment for some investors to buy into Coinbase's stock: as line item trends suggest, the company's line item efficiency is rising and poised to soar higher. Of course, Robinhood's broadness of market instrument accessibility is no small thing, either.

All in all, neither Coinbase nor Robinhood is definitively better or worse than the other. The weary American consumer's behavioral outlook is the trillion-dollar question.

data.title: Hudson Pacific Properties: Stick A Fork In It
data.description:
When You Come To A Fork In The Road, Take It.

Source: Yogi Berra

We have been handing out Sell ratings on Hudson Pacific Properties Inc. (NYSE:HPP) like candy for over a year now.

It has been a fruitful exercise and the stock is down a tad since our first red article.

HPP just reported its Q4 numbers and also went over what it expects for 2025. We go over those and tell you why the red streak is not coming to an end.

Q4-2024

Over the past year, we have repeatedly spoken about convergence. The convergence between the occupancy rate of HPP and the primary city where it has its office properties, San Francisco.

You have to remember that HPP's vacancy rate is still sub-20% and its primary city's vacancy rate is now, over 35%!

Source: Downgrading Common And Preferreds To A Sell

So it comes as no surprise when quarter after quarter, HPP's occupancy drifts lower. This one was no different. In service, percentage occupied dropped from 79.1% to 78.3%. This was despite a robust set of leases executed. We say "robust" here, as it takes a lot of effort to lease half a million square feet of lease with this level of challenges. But the actual GAAP and cash rents were even lower on those.

When you go further down the picture shown above, there are four major things you have to note.

1) Funds from operations (FFO) is down from 14 cents to 11 cents year over year.

2) Adjusted funds from operations (AFFO) is now down to just 2 cents per share.

3) Debt to EBITDA is up to a whopping 11.1X.

4) Weighted average debt to maturity is down to just 2.6 years.

The combination of 1 and 2 should tell you that the "low valuation" you saw 18 months back was a mirage. That was when the stock was more than twice the price it is today and traded at a price to FFO of 4. The combination of 3 and 4 should tell you that you are investing in an extremely distressed company. You could possibly blow up triple net lease assets with a debt to EBITDA of 11.1X, let alone office assets. That is why Global Net Lease (GNL) has been selling properties left, right and center to keep debt to EBITDA under, wait for it, 8X. This all would look extremely gruesome, except that we have not reached the guidance portion of the information released.

Guidance

For the full year 2025, same property net operating income (NOI), is expected to decline by 13%.

13% decline is astronomical for REITs on a same store basis. What is fascinating about this is that it comes after a year where NOI already declined just as much. Well, actually it declined slightly more in 2024, but you get what we are trying to say.

HPP is telling you that another $50 million of same property NOI is going to poof here. That should put Debt to EBITDA comfortably over 12.0X and possibly at 13.0X, depending on which assets are sold during the year and what they fetch. If you just knock off $50 million from the 2024 full year AFFO, you land up with $17 million in residual AFFO for 2025. But that is fairly simplistic and likely extremely wrong. For one, capex and tenant improvements and leasing commissions are all likely to increase in 2025. If Q4 is any guide, the tenants are going to be demanding a couple of pounds of flesh to sign any lease.

The crux of the story is that we expect negative AFFO for 2025, and it should be deep in the red zone.

Verdict

This all continues until covenants break, and no further relief can be obtained. Even assuming they can get through those with waivers, we really don't see how refinancing the 2025 and 2026 maturities at market rates leaves anything left for the equity.

HPP is currently paying 4.7% on its debt.

Amongst the tradeable bonds on Interactive Brokers, the nearest maturity has a 7.25% YTM and the last one is over 10%.

These are in an era of the tightest/lowest spreads of credit to Treasury bonds. So in any kind of financial turbulence, you should expect double digit yields if HPP wants to refinance its debt past 2026.

On the macro front, there was a single green shoot, with San Francisco recording its first quarter of positive absorption.

We will note though, that the 36.5% vacancy rate (63.5% occupancy rate) remains a daunting challenge for HPP with its 78.3% occupancy rate. A quarter of its leases come up for renewal in the next two years.

We continue to rate the shares as a Sell.

Hudson Pacific Properties, Inc. 4.750% CUM PFD C (NYSE:HPP.PR.C)

While the common shares have gone pretty much exactly where we expected them to, HPP.PR.C has hung on over the last year. This, of course, has likely emboldened those shareholders into thinking that there is something special about those shares. For us, we cannot see any scenario where the common and preferred equity are not completely obliterated over the next 3 years. We don't think even the bonds come out unscathed, despite trading at sizeable discounts to par. We rate this a Strong Sell as when the hammer drops, it will be a sizeable sudden hit.
